content:
Software Development

Software Engineering

Data Engineering

Artificial Intelligence

Data Science

35 min read
Just now

--

The two “ordering rules” that sit underneath undo buttons, task schedulers, message brokers, and almost every distributed system you’ve ever used — explained simply, with a data engineering lens

A Quick Recap Before We Continue
In Part 1 of this series, we built a foundation with Arrays and Linked Lists — the two most basic ways to store a collection of items in memory. We learned that arrays give you instant (O(1)) access by index but pay a heavy price (O(n)) for inserting or deleting in the middle, while linked lists flip that trade-off: cheap insertion and deletion, but no shortcuts for random access.
Today, we take the next logical step. Stacks and Queues are not brand-new ways of storing data — they are restricted ways of using the arrays and linked lists we already understand. A stack or a queue can be built using either an array or a linked list underneath. What makes them special isn’t the storage mechanism — it’s the rule they enforce about the order in which you’re allowed to add and remove items.
This single idea — restricting how you’re allowed to interact with your data — turns out to be one of the most powerful and widely used concepts in all of computing. Undo buttons, browser back buttons, task schedulers, message queues, deadlock detection, recursive function calls, breadth-first traversal of dependency graphs — every single one of these rests on top of a stack or a queue.
And, as we’ll see throughout this article, so does a huge amount of real-world data engineering infrastructure — often in places you’d never expect.
Let’s dig in.

Part 1: Stacks
What Is a Stack, Really?
Picture a stack of plates in a cafeteria. When the kitchen staff washes a new plate, they place it on top of the existing stack. When a customer needs a plate, they take the one from the top — not from the bottom, and not from somewhere in the middle. The very last plate placed on the stack is the very first one to be picked up.
This behavior has a name: LIFO — Last In, First Out.
A stack is a data structure that only allows you to do two things:

• Push — add a new element to the top of the stack

• Pop — remove the element currently at the top of the stack
That’s it. You cannot reach into the middle of a stack and pull out an item without first removing everything above it. This restriction sounds limiting, but it’s precisely this simplicity that makes stacks so useful, predictable, and fast.
A third operation, Peek (sometimes called Top), lets you look at the element currently on top without removing it — useful when you need to check what’s there before deciding whether to pop it.
Visually, here’s what pushing three values onto a stack looks like:
Push 10: [10]
Push 20: [20]
[10]
Push 30: [30]
[20]
[10]If we now call pop(), we get 30 back (the most recently added item), and the stack becomes:
[20]
[10]How Stacks Are Implemented Underneath
As mentioned, a stack isn’t a brand-new storage mechanism — it’s a set of rules layered on top of either an array or a linked list.
Array-based stack: You keep a variable (often called top) tracking the index of the last inserted element. push() increments top and places the new element there; pop() reads the element at top and decrements it. Because both operations only ever touch the "end" of the array — never the middle — both push and pop are O(1) operations (amortized O(1) if the underlying array needs to dynamically resize, exactly as we discussed with dynamic arrays in Part 1).
Linked-list-based stack: You treat the head of the linked list as the “top” of the stack. push() becomes "insert at head" and pop() becomes "remove at head" — both of which we already established are O(1) operations for a singly linked list, with no traversal required. This is actually a very natural fit, since a stack never needs random access into the middle — exactly the one thing linked lists are bad at.
Either implementation gives you O(1) push and pop, which is why stacks are considered one of the cheapest, most efficient data structures you can use, provided your access pattern genuinely only needs “last in, first out” behavior.
A Simple Code Example
class Stack:
def __init__(self):
self.items = [] def push(self, item):
self.items.append(item) # O(1) amortized def pop(self):
if self.is_empty():
raise IndexError("pop from an empty stack")
return self.items.pop() # O(1) — removes from the end def peek(self):
if self.is_empty():
raise IndexError("peek from an empty stack")
return self.items[-1] def is_empty(self):
return len(self.items) == 0 def size(self):
return len(self.items)
# Usage
s = Stack()
s.push(10)
s.push(20)
s.push(30)
print(s.pop()) # 30
print(s.peek()) # 20
print(s.size()) # 2Notice we implemented this using Python’s built-in list, calling append() for push and pop() (with no index, which defaults to the last element) for pop. Both operations touch only the end of the underlying array, which is exactly why they're fast — we're never shifting elements around, unlike the array insertion problems we discussed in Part 1.
Core Stack Operations and Their Time Complexity
Operation What It Means Time Complexity Push Add an element to the top O(1) amortized Pop Remove the top element O(1) Peek/Top View the top element without removing it O(1) Search Check if a value exists anywhere in the stack O(n) IsEmpty Check if the stack has zero elements O(1)
Notice that search is still O(n) — a stack doesn’t give you any special power to look things up quickly. Its entire value proposition is the guaranteed, cheap, ordered access to just one end.
A Quick Big-O Refresher, Applied to Stacks and Queues
If you’re joining this series starting here, it’s worth pausing to re-anchor on what these complexity labels actually mean, because they’ll come up constantly for the rest of this article.
Big-O notation describes how the cost of an operation grows as the size of the input grows — it’s a description of a trend, not a stopwatch measurement. O(1) means the operation takes the same amount of time no matter how large the collection is — pushing onto a stack of 10 items costs the same as pushing onto a stack of 10 million items, because you’re only ever touching one specific spot (the top). O(n) means the cost grows in direct proportion to the size of the collection — searching for a value buried somewhere inside a stack means, in the worst case, checking every single element.
This matters enormously in practice. Consider a validation pipeline that pushes every incoming record onto a stack, and then, for every new record, checks whether it already exists somewhere in that stack (an O(n) search) before deciding whether to push it. If you’re processing a million records, and each one triggers a full O(n) scan through an ever-growing stack, you’ve accidentally built an O(n²) pipeline — a million records times up to a million comparisons each, which, as we discussed conceptually in Part 1, can balloon into trillions of operations and bring your pipeline to a crawl. The fix in a case like this usually isn’t to abandon the stack — it’s to pair it with a hash-based lookup structure (something we’ll cover in depth in the next article) specifically so “does this exist already” stays O(1), while the stack continues handling the LIFO ordering it’s actually good at. This is a preview of a recurring theme in this series: real systems rarely use just one data structure in isolation — they combine structures so that each one is doing exactly the job it’s naturally suited for.
Multiple Stacks Working Together
One underappreciated but very common pattern is using two stacks in tandem to solve a problem that a single stack can’t handle on its own. We already saw a hint of this with the “Undo/Redo” example: one stack tracks actions that can be undone, and a second stack tracks actions that have been undone and can be redone. When you perform a brand-new action after undoing something, the redo stack is typically cleared entirely — this is because that “alternate future” is no longer valid once you’ve branched off in a new direction, a subtlety that trips up a lot of naive undo/redo implementations.
This “two stacks, each with a distinct responsibility” pattern extends further than UI undo buttons. Consider a data validation pipeline that needs to support “rolling back” the last N validation rule changes during a testing phase, while also allowing a “re-apply” of a rolled-back rule if a tester changes their mind — the exact same two-stack pattern applies directly, just in a governance and rule-management context instead of a text editor.
Monotonic Stacks: A Specialized but Powerful Pattern
One more stack variation worth knowing about, because it shows up constantly in both interviews and real analytical workloads, is the monotonic stack — a stack that is kept, at all times, either strictly increasing or strictly decreasing from bottom to top. Whenever a new element would violate that ordering, elements are popped off until the ordering is restored again, and then the new element is pushed.
This sounds abstract, so let’s ground it with an example: imagine you have a stream of hourly temperature readings, and for every hour, you want to know how many hours you’ll have to wait until a warmer temperature occurs. A naive approach would compare every reading against every future reading — an O(n²) approach. A monotonic (decreasing) stack solves this in a single O(n) pass: you keep a stack of “temperatures we’re still waiting to see something warmer than,” and every time a new reading comes in that’s warmer than the temperature on top of the stack, you pop it off (you’ve just found its answer) and repeat until the stack’s top is warmer than the current reading, then push the current reading on. This single-pass, O(n) pattern — instead of the naive O(n²) comparison — is a direct, practical illustration of how understanding stack mechanics deeply can turn a slow brute-force approach into an efficient one, and it generalizes well beyond temperature: the same pattern is used for “next higher/lower value” problems in time-series anomaly detection, stock price analysis, and log-based event correlation in monitoring pipelines.
Where Stacks Show Up in Everyday Software
Before jumping into data engineering specifics, it’s worth grounding this in a few universally familiar examples, because they build the right intuition:

• The “Undo” button in almost any application (a text editor, a design tool, a spreadsheet) is a stack. Every action you take gets pushed onto an “undo stack.” Pressing “Undo” pops the most recent action and reverses it. If you then press “Redo,” that undone action often gets pushed onto a separate “redo stack” — a clean, elegant use of two stacks working together.

• The browser’s “Back” button works the same way — every page you visit gets pushed onto a history stack, and pressing “Back” pops the most recent page.

• Function calls in every programming language are managed using something literally called the “call stack.” When a function calls another function, the calling function’s state gets pushed onto the call stack; when the called function finishes, its state is popped off, and control returns to exactly where it left off. This is also why deeply recursive functions (like an unbounded recursive traversal of a huge tree structure) can cause a “stack overflow” error — you’re pushing more frames onto the call stack than the system has memory reserved for it.

• Checking for balanced parentheses/brackets in code editors and compilers ({[()]} is balanced; {[(])} is not) is a classic stack problem: push every opening bracket, and every time you see a closing bracket, check that it matches the most recently pushed opening bracket (pop and compare).

Stacks in Data Engineering — Where This Actually Shows Up
1. Query Execution Plans and Nested Expression Evaluation
